question: What is the running time of the Floyd-Warshall algorithm?
answer:
# Running Time of the Floyd-Warshall Algorithm

Based on the pseudo-code provided in the course material, the Floyd-Warshall algorithm has the following structure:

```text
void Floyd-Warshall(n, W)
{
    D^(0) ← W
    for (k = 1; k <= n; k++)           // outer loop: n iterations
        for (i = 1; i <= n; i++)        // middle loop: n iterations
            for (j = 1; j <= n; j++)    // inner loop: n iterations
                d_ij^(k) ← min(d_ij^(k-1), d_ik^(k-1) + d_kj^(k-1))
}
```

## Analysis

The algorithm consists of **three nested `for` loops**, each running from 1 to n (where n is the number of vertices in the graph). Inside the innermost loop, a single `min` operation is performed, which takes **constant time Θ(1)**.

Counting the total number of operations:

| Loop | Number of iterations |
|------|---------------------|
| Outer (k) | n |
| Middle (i) | n |
| Inner (j) | n |

The total number of operations is:

$$n \times n \times n = n^3$$

## Running Time

$$\boxed{\Theta(n^3)}$$

The running time of the Floyd-Warshall algorithm is **Θ(n³)** — cubic in the number of vertices n. This makes it a polynomial-time algorithm, which is why it is widely used for the all-pairs shortest path problem on dense graphs.

chunks.section_title: Limitations of Experiments when evaluating the running time of an algorithm
chunks.content:
- Experimental evaluation of running time is very useful but
  - It is necessary to implement the algorithm, which may be difficult
  - Results may not be indicative of the running time on other inputs not included in the experiment
  - In order to compare two algorithms, the same hardware and software environments must be used

➔ We need: **Theoretical Analysis of Running Time**

- Uses a pseudo-code description of the algorithm instead of an implementation
- Characterizes running time as a function of the input size, $n$
- Takes into account all possible inputs
- Allows us to evaluate the speed of an algorithm independent of the hardware/software environment (Changing the hardware/software environment affects the running time by a constant factor, but does not alter the growth rate of the running time)

$T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n,\ \ T(1) = 4$

| Iteration | Recursion tree level / cost |
|---|---|
| 0 | $T(n)$, cost $n$ |
| 1 | $T(n/2) + T(n/2)$, cost $n/2 + n/2$ |
| 2 | $4 \cdot T(n/4)$

| Step | Operation | Queue Q | Newly visited |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | From $s$: can go to $b$ and $e$ | $\{b,e\}$ | $b,e$ |
| 2 | Dequeue($Q$): remove $b$ | $\{e\}$ | $a,c,k$ |
| 3 | From $b$: can go to $a,c,k,s$ | $\{e,a,c,k\}$ | $a,c,k$ |
| 4 | Dequeue($Q$): remove $e$ | $\{a,c,k\}$ | none |
| 5 | From $e$: can go to $k,s$ | $\{a,c,k\}$ | none |
| 6 | Dequeue($Q$): remove $a$ | $\{c,k\}$ | none |
| 7 | From $a$: can go to $b,c$ | $\{c,k\}$ | none |
| 8 | Dequeue($Q$): remove $c$ | $\{k\}$ | $d,g$ |
| 9 | From $c$: can go to $a,b,d,g,k$ | $\{k,d,g\}$ | $d,g$ |
| 10 | Dequeue($Q$): remove $k$ | $\{d,g\}$ | none |
| 11 | From $k$: can go to $b,c,e$ | $\{d,g\}$ | none |
| 12 | Dequeue($Q$): remove $d$ | $\{g\}$ | none |
| 13 | From $d$: can go to $c,g$ | $\{g\}$ | none |
| 14 | Dequeue($Q$): remove $g$ | empty | none |
| 15 | From $g$: can go to $d,c$ | empty | none |

- Q is now empty. All vertices of the graph were visited. Algorithm is finished.
